coredata.dc:description: This study demonstrates the synthesis and application of nitrogen-doped tannin carbon dots (TCDs) derived from condensed tannin of grapestone through a single-step hydrothermal method. The TCDs were employed to enhance the electrical conductivity, active site availability, structural stability, and charge storage mechanisms of NiCo-LDH composite electrodes. Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the incorporation of TCDs increases the density of states (DOS) near the Fermi level, thereby improving charge transport properties and contributing to the enhanced cycling stability. In a three-electrode system, the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode exhibits an exceptional specific capacitance of 1804.2F g−1 at 1 A g−1, with a capacitance retention of 77.4 % at a high current densities of 10 A g−1. As button cell of asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) assembled with tannin-based activated carbon (TAC600-4) as the negative electrode and NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 as the positive electrode, the device demonstrates a maximum energy density of 87 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1, and maintains a high energy density of 61.6 Wh kg−1 even at an ultra-high power density of 8000 W kg−1, significantly surpassing devices composed solely of NiCo-LDH. Durability assessments further revealed 78.6 % capacitance retention and 100.9 of coulombic efficiency after 10,000 cycles, underscoring its applicability in high-performance energy storage. This study not only advances the synthesis of carbon dots from sustainable tannin sources but also provides valuable insights into optimizing the specific capacitance, rate capability, and cycling durability of LDH-based supercapacitors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis schematic diagram of NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite materials.

(a) TEM image of TCDs sample; Microstructure of the samples: (b) NiCo-LDH; (c) NiCo-LDH@TCDs1.5; (d) NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0; (e) NiCo-LDH@TCDs4.5; TEM images of (f-g) NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0; (h) HRTEM image of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 showing lattice fringes; (k) SAED pattern of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0; (i) EDS mapping of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0, showing elemental distribution: C (red), N (green), O (purple), Ni (blue), and Co (yellow). Data in (h) are averaged from three independent measurements, with decimal precision corresponding to experimental uncertainty.
(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms; (b) Pore size distribution (PSD) curves; (c) XRD patterns of NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs samples; (d) FTIR spectra of TCDs, NiCo-LDH, and NiCo-LDH@TCDs samples.
(a) XPS survey; High-resolution spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) Ni 2p, and (f) Co 2p in the NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite.
DOSs projected onto the different atomic orbitals of (a) NiCo-LDH and (b) NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites. The K ion adsorption capacities of both (c) NiCo-LDH and (d) NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites.

Electrochemical performance of NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite electrodes: (a) CV curves at 5 mV s−1; (b) GCD curves at 1 A g−1; (c) CV curves of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode at different scan rates; (d) GCD curves of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 at different current densities; (e) Relationship between specific capacitance and current density for NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite electrodes; (f) EIS fitting diagram with equivalent circuit model; (g) Capacitive contribution (green area) in the CV curve of the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1; (h) Proportion of capacitive and diffusion-controlled contributions to the total current at various scan rates for the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode. Data in (e) and (h) are averaged from three independent measurements, with decimal precision corresponding to experimental uncertainty.

Electrochemical performance of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0//TAC600-4 asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC): (a) CV curves under different voltage windows at 5 mV s−1; (b) GCD curves at different voltage windows at 0.5 A g−1; (c) CV curves at various scan rates; (d) GCD curves at different current densities; (e) Ragone plot showing energy and power densities, the insert is the asymmetric supercapacitor device in powering an LED bulb; (f) Cycling stability test of the ASC over 10,000 cycles. Data in (e) and (f) are averaged from three independent measurements, with decimal precision corresponding to experimental uncertainty.
Pore structure parameters of NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs.
The performance of the supercapacitors obtained in this work is compared with similar devices reported in the literature.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study demonstrates the synthesis and application of nitrogen-doped tannin carbon dots (TCDs) derived from condensed tannin of grapestone through a single-step hydrothermal method. The TCDs were employed to enhance the electrical conductivity, active site availability, structural stability, and charge storage mechanisms of NiCo-LDH composite electrodes. Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the incorporation of TCDs increases the density of states (DOS) near the Fermi level, thereby improving charge transport properties and contributing to the enhanced cycling stability. In a three-electrode system, the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode exhibits an exceptional specific capacitance of 1804.2F g−1 at 1 A g−1, with a capacitance retention of 77.4 % at a high current densities of 10 A g−1. As button cell of asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) assembled with tannin-based activated carbon (TAC600-4) as the negative electrode and NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 as the positive electrode, the device demonstrates a maximum energy density of 87 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1, and maintains a high energy density of 61.6 Wh kg−1 even at an ultra-high power density of 8000 W kg−1, significantly surpassing devices composed solely of NiCo-LDH. Durability assessments further revealed 78.6 % capacitance retention and 100.9 of coulombic efficiency after 10,000 cycles, underscoring its applicability in high-performance energy storage. This study not only advances the synthesis of carbon dots from sustainable tannin sources but also provides valuable insights into optimizing the specific capacitance, rate capability, and cycling durability of LDH-based supercapacitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing reliance on electronic devices and electric vehicles has spurred an urgent demand for high-performance energy storage systems. Among various electrochemical energy storage devices, supercapacitors (SCs) have emerged as promising candidates due to their high power density, rapid charge–discharge capability, and long cycle life [1]. Supercapacitors are broadly categorized into electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors. EDLCs rely on non-Faradaic ion adsorption at the electrode/electrolyte interface, offering limited specific capacitance and energy density [2]. In contrast, pseudocapacitors utilize fast and reversible surface redox reactions to store energy, achieving higher specific capacitance and energy density [3]. However, pseudocapacitors often face challenges such as poor rate capability and insufficient cycling stability, driving the need for advanced electrode materials with enhanced electrochemical properties.

Typical pseudocapacitive electrode materials encompass conductive polymers, and also the oxides, carbides, nitrides, sulfides, and hydroxides of transition metal [4–8]. Recently, layered double hydroxides (LDHs), particularly nickel–cobalt LDH (NiCo-LDH), have attracted significant attention due to their two-dimensional nanostructures, cost-effectiveness, environmentally benign synthesis, and high theoretical specific capacitance (∼3000F g−1) [10,[96,10]. Despite these advantages, practical applications of NiCo-LDH are hindered by its low intrinsic electrical conductivity, structural instability, and agglomeration tendency [3,11]. Strategies such as compositing LDHs with conductive materials (e.g., graphene [12], carbon nanotubes [13], and MXenes [14]) have been explored to improve performance. Nevertheless, most of these approaches only establish weak physical interactions between LDH and the conductive matrix, resulting in poor cycling stability as the LDH gradually detaches from the substrate during repeated charge–discharge cycles [8]. Additionally, the limited ion diffusion pathways in conventional NiCo-LDH structures restrict their rate performance.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, offer exceptional chemical stability, environmental compatibility, low cost, and non-toxicity. Rich in diverse functional groups, CDs exhibit unique electronic properties that enhance conductivity and interfacial activity in electrochemical systems [15]. Recent studies demonstrate that CDs can induce tailored morphologies (e.g., flower-like, spherical, tremella-like structures) in composite materials, optimizing their physicochemical properties for specific applications [15]. When integrated into composites, CDs expand ion transport interfaces between electrodes and electrolytes while improving conductivity through edge effects and quantum confinement, thereby boosting specific capacitance, capacity, and cycling stability. Notably, CDs can regulate composite synthesis, modulate physicochemical properties, and stabilize LDH structures, significantly enhancing both specific capacitance and cycle life. However, challenges remain: CD synthesis methods vary widely, yielding materials with inconsistent sizes, shapes, and functional group distributions. Moreover, low synthesis yields and high production costs hinder industrial scalability. Thus, developing sustainable, cost-effective CD synthesis routes is critical for advancing scalable energy storage technologies.

Recent advances in supercapacitor research have highlighted the potential of biomass-derived materials, particularly tannin, as electrode enhancers. Tannin, a naturally abundant polyphenolic compound, has been widely utilized in adhesives, biomass foams, aerogels, and activated carbons due to its unique chemical structure and functional versatility [16–18]. Composed of flavonoid oligomers with varying polymerization degrees (Fig. 1 ), tannin exhibits high chemical stability and abundant functional groups (e.g., –OH, C-O-C) [17], making it an ideal precursor for synthesizing advanced materials such as carbon dots (CDs). Tannin-derived CDs are typically synthesized via eco-friendly hydrothermal methods, leveraging its oxygen-rich functional groups to generate porous, electrochemically active carbon materials. Surface functional groups on CDs enhance conductivity, wettability, and electrolyte–electrode interactions, promoting efficient ion transport. Nitrogen doping during synthesis further modifies the electronic structure of CDs, introducing pseudocapacitive mechanisms and additional active sites (Table S1) [19], thereby improving specific capacitance and energy density.

In this work, we synthesize nitrogen-doped tannin carbon dots (TCDs) from renewable tannin precursors using a single-step hydrothermal method, offering a sustainable and cost-effective alternative to conventional CD synthesis. The integration of TCDs into NiCo-LDH modulates its morphology, forming hierarchical porous nanostructures that enhance electrolyte penetration and charge storage efficiency. Functional groups on TCDs strengthen interfacial bonding between NiCo-LDH layers, improving structural stability during cycling. Combining electrochemical analysis with Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations, we elucidate the electronic structure, charge transport mechanisms, and electrochemical stability of NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites at the atomic level. The optimized NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode delivers an ultrahigh specific capacitance of 1804.2F g−1 at 1 A g−1, surpassing pure NiCo-LDH and most reported LDH-based electrodes. An asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) assembled with NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 (positive electrode) and tannin-based activated carbon (TAC600-4, negative electrode) achieves an exceptional energy density of 87 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1, while maintaining 61.6 Wh kg−1 at 8000 W kg−1. This study not only provides a scalable and sustainable strategy for high-performance electrode materials but also demonstrates the transformative potential of biomass-derived carbon dots in next-generation energy storage systems.

Analytical grade tannin (brown powder, condensed flavonoid oligomers, CAS 1401–55-4) was provided by SAS Sofralab (Magenta, France). Ethylenediamine (≥99.5 %), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, ≥98 %), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, ≥99 %) urea, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), and ethylene glycol (EG, ≥99.5 %) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Analytical grade acetylene black (conductive agent, >99.9 %), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, ≥99.5 %) were purchased from Tianjin Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm resistivity) was used throughout the experiments.

Tannin carbon dots (TCDs) were synthesized via a single-step hydrothermal process. Briefly, 0.5 g of tannin was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring at 500 rpm for 10 min to ensure complete dissolution. Subsequently, 0.5 g of ethylenediamine was added as a pH adjuster and complexing agent, followed by the addition of 10 mL of 30 % hydrogen peroxide solution as an oxidant. The mixture was stirred at the same speed for an additional 20 min. The mixture was then transferred into a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and reacted at 260℃ for 8 h. Then, the pale yellow carbon dot mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 r min−1 for 10 min to remove unreacted tannin powder and other impurities using a 0.25 μm filter. The liquid was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) in DI water for 72 h and then freeze-dried at −50℃ and 0.1 mbar to obtain TCDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites were prepared by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, 1.5 mg, 3 mg, or 4.5 mg of TCDs were uniformly dispersed in 30 vol% ethylene glycol (EG) solution under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, 0.291 g each of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and Co(NO3)2·6H2O were added to the solution, followed by vigorous stirring for 20 min to ensure thorough mixing. Next, 1.5 g of urea was introduced into the mixture, which was then stirred vigorously for an additional 40 min. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor and subjected to a microwave-assisted hydrothermal reaction at 120℃ for 30 min using an XH-800S microwave hydrothermal instrument (Xianghu Company, Beijing, China). After the reaction, the NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite materials were separated from the solvent by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, washed three times with 30 % EG solution to remove residual impurities, and subsequently dried in a vacuum oven at 60℃ for 12 h. The resulting materials were labeled as NiCo-LDH@TCDsX (X = 1.5, 3.0, 4.5) based on the amount of TCDs added. For comparison, pure NiCo-LDH was synthesized under identical conditions but without the addition of TCDs. A schematic representation of the synthesis process is provided in Fig. 1.

The microstructure and morphology of the samples were characterized using field emission gun scanning electron microscopy (FEG-SEM Hitachi S3400) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100). The crystal structure, chemical composition, and chemical valence states of the samples were characterized using an X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8, Bragg-Brentano Geometry), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (VERTEX 70, FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AXIS Ultra, XPS). The specific surface area (SBET), total pore volume (V0.99), micropore volume (VDR), and mesopore volume (Vmeso) of the samples were characterized using an physical adsorption instrument (ASAP 2020, micromeritics).f.

Electrochemical performance was tested using a Zennium electrochemical workstation (Zahner, Germany) with a three-electrode system. Prior to testing, working electrodes were prepared by mixing the sample with acetylene black and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in a mass ratio of 8:1:1 in a mortar, adding a certain amount of N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and grinding until the mixture was uniformly dispersed. The suspension was evenly coated on a 1 × 2 cm nickel foam, with a coating area of 1 × 1 cm, and the prepared electrode sheets were dried in an 85℃ constant temperature vacuum oven for 12 h to obtain dry working electrodes. A saturated calomel electrode was used as the reference electrode, a platinum sheet as the counter electrode, and 6 M KOH as the electrolyte to construct the three-electrode system.

Using NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite materials as the positive electrode and tannin-based activated carbon (TAC600-4) as the negative electrode (the detailed preparation process and electrochemical performance of TAC600-4 are provided in Supporting Information S1), an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was assembled in a CR2032 coin-cell configuration with 6 M KOH solution as the electrolyte. The calculation methods and formulas for both the three- and two-electrode systems can be find in the supporting informations.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the projector augmented plane-wave (PAW) method [20] as implemented in the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) [21,22]. The exchange–correlation potential was described by the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [23] with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional [24]. A plane-wave cutoff energy of 500 eV was used for all calculations. Structural optimizations were conducted using a Monkhorst-Pack k-point grid of 3 × 3 × 2 [25], while electronic structure calculations employed a denser grid of 5 × 5 × 3. The self-consistent field (SCF) convergence criterion was set to 10-5 eV, and atomic positions were relaxed until the residual forces were below 0.01 eV Å-1. The adsorption energy (ΔE ads) of potassium ions (K+) on NiCo-LDH or NiCo-LDH@TCDs was calculated using the following equation:
ΔE ads = E NiCo-LDH+K–E NiCo-LDH–E K.
where E NiCoLDH+K is the total energy of K ion adsorbed NiCo-LDH or NiCo-LDH@TCDs, E NiCo-LDH represents the energy of the NiCo-LDH or NiCo-LDH@TCDs, and E K is the energy of an isolated K+ ion.

To validate the successful synthesis of tannin carbon dots (TCDs), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to analyze their morphology. As shown in Fig. 2 a, TCDs exhibit a quasi-spherical morphology with uniform distribution and an average size of ∼ 2.82 nm [29]. Further characterization, including N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and X-ray diffraction (XRD), was conducted to elucidate the intrinsic properties of TCDs (Supplementary Information, Fig. S1-S2). The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms confirm the mesoporous nature of TCDs, while XRD patterns indicate their amorphous carbon structure. The microstructure of pure NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites was investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 2b-e. Pure NiCo-LDH (Fig. 2b) exhibites a a three-dimensional spherical structure composed of disordered stacks of nanoneedle/nanosheet LDH. Upon the addition of 1.5 mg of TCDs, the LDH structures undergo significant agglomeration, forming irregular stacks (Fig. 2c) with limited redox-active sites, which may impair capacitive performance. Increasing the TCDs content to 3.0 mg resulted in numerous nanorod-like LDH clusters that formed more regular needle-shaped spherical structures. These structures exhibit increased surface defects, which enhance the contact area with the electrolyte and provide abundant redox-active sites, thereby improving capacitive performance [26]. Further increasing the TCDs content to 4.5 mg, the material’s structure primarily consisted of a mixture of floral patterns formed by abundant sheet-like LDH and irregular spherical structures formed by disordered stacking of mainly nanosheet LDH. These observations suggest that TCDs concentration plays a critical role in modulating the growth of NiCo-LDH, likely due to TCDs acting as nucleation centers and interacting with functional groups to drive distinct morphologies. Previous studies have demonstrated that the morphology and surface chemistry of nucleating materials significantly influence LDH growth [27]. Thus, TCDs, with their abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and active sites, effectively tailor the surface morphology and structure of the composite. Among the synthesized materials, NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 exhibits the most favorable porous structure, offering an optimal balance of conductivity, electrochemical accessibility, and structural integrity.

Further insights into the microstructure of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 were obtained through TEM analysis. Fig. 2f confirms the presence of regular nanoneedle-shaped spherical structures. High-magnification TEM (Fig. 2g) reveals TCDs embedded within the LDH matrix, verifying the successful synthesis of the NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) analysis (Fig. 2h) reveal lattice spacings of 0.72 nm and 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (003) and (100) planes of NiCo-LDH and TCDs, respectively [28]. The SAED pattern displays three distinct diffraction rings, indexed to the (003), (101), and (110) planes of NiCo-LDH [29]. Additionally, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping (Fig. 2i) demonstrates the uniform distribution of C, N, O, Ni, and Co elements across the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 composite, confirming its chemical and structural homogeneity.

Pore structure plays a critical role in determining the interaction between the electrolyte and electrode material, as well as the availability of redox-active sites. Fig. 3 a and 3b illustrate the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distribution (PSD) curves for NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites. As shown in Fig. 3a, all materials exhibit Type IV isotherms, characterized by low adsorption at low pressures and distinct H3-type hysteresis loops, indicating a predominantly mesoporous structure with minor contributions from micropores [30]. Fig. 3b further confirmed this observation, showing that the pore sizes of all materials predominantly fall within less than 50 nm. The detailed PSD curves reveal that for NiCo-LDH, micropores are primarily within the 1.3–1.6 nm range with extremely small amount, still mainly mesoporous. While for NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites, the micropore size shifts to the range of 0.4 to 2 nm, and with a notably higher pore volume. Interestingly, for NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites, pores smaller than 1 nm are quite obvious, with NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 showing the highest concentration. They can significantly enhance the specific surface area, thereby providing more active sites. Additionally, these micropores improve the electrolyte ions’adsorption capacity on the material’s surface, promoting efficient ion utilization within the electrode material, ultimately boosting the overall electrochemical performance. For mesopores, the PSD curves are basically overlapping, indicating that the pore volume of the mesopores has changed little. To quantitatively compare pore structure differences, specific surface area (SBET), total pore volume (V0.99), micropore volume (VDR), and mesopore volume (Vmeso) were calculated from the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, as shown in Table 1 . The NiCo-LDH@TCDs samples show a significant decrease in the parameters of micropore average pore size (L0) and mesopore volumes, compared to pure NiCo-LDH. However, the micropore pore volume and specific surface area increased noteworthy. The addition of carbon dots significantly enhances the micropore volume and surface area of NiCo-LDH due to several synergistic effects. The addition of carbon dots, with their nanoscale size of 2.82 nm from TEM analysis, facilitates the formation of micropores by interacting with the layered structure of LDH or by filling and stabilizing existing pore networks, leading to a significant increase in micropore volume [19]. Furthermore, during the synthesis process, carbon dots may act as a pore-forming template, guiding the development of micropores and alters the LDH micro-morphology. Morphological changes-such as the formation of regular needle-shaped spherical structures—often correlate with increased surface area, as these shapes typically provide more surface area per unit volume than sheet-like structures [31]. The microporous structure generated by the incorporation of TCDs enhances electrolyte penetration, improving ion accessibility and charge storage. This characteristic gives TCDs a significant advantage over carbon nanotube-derived CDs (CNTDs), which typically exhibit a more compact structure with fewer accessible micropores, thereby limiting efficient ion diffusion. Among all NiCo-LDH@TCDs samples, NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 exhibits the highest specific surface area, which can be attributed to its unique regular needle-like and flower-like nanostructures characteristics observed in the SEM images. This structural advantage highlights the potential of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 for superior performance in electrochemical applications.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to characterize the composition, crystal structure, and purity of the NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite. As shown in Fig. 3c, all samples display distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ = 12.3°, 24.8°, 33.1°, and 59°, corresponding to the typical (003), (006), (101), and (110) planes of NiCo-LDH [32]. These results are consistent with the SAED patterns observed in the TEM analysis (Fig. 2), confirming the successful synthesis of NiCo-LDH under microwave-assisted hydrothermal conditions [33]. Notably, the XRD pattern of the NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites does not display characteristic peaks of TCDs, likely due to the minimal amount of TCDs present in the composite. Fig. 3d presents the Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis results for TCDs, NiCo-LDH, and NiCo-LDH@TCDs. The absorption peaks of TCDs at 1120 cm−1 and 1204 cm−1 correspond to C-N and C-O bonds [34], while the peak at 1717 cm−1 represents C=O [35], and the broad absorption peak at 3500 cm−1 is mainly attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH and N-H, indicating the successful preparation of N-doped tannin carbon dots. The FTIR spectrum of NiCo-LDH features absorption peaks at 3636.5 cm−1 and 3499 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H bonds of water molecules attached to the surface of LDH [36], and the strong absorption peak at 2241 cm−1 due to CN stretching vibrations, indicating the incomplete decomposition of ure a [37]. The peak at 1630 cm−1 is attributed to O-H-O, representing the deformation mode of water molecules between LDH layers [38]. The two peaks at 1485 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 can be attributed to CO3 2– and NO3 – [39]. Moreover, the two peaks appearing at 465 and 521 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Ni-O, Co-O, and Ni-OH, Co-OH [40], further confirming the successful synthesis of NiCo-LDH, consistent with the XRD results. These results confirm not only the successful synthesis of carbon dots, NiCo-LDH, and their composites but also demonstrate that the inclusion of TCDs does not significantly alter the crystal structure or surface chemical characteristics of LDH.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further employed to investigate the elemental composition and chemical states of the NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite materials, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4a) reveal distinct peaks attributed to Ni 2p, Co 2p, O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s, aligning with the elemental mapping results of NiCo-LDH@TCDs in Fig. 2, confirming the expected elemental composition. High-resolution spectra for each element were fitted using the Gaussian fitting method, with detailed results shown in Fig. 4b-f. In Fig. 4b, the C 1 s spectrum exhibits peaks at 283.8 eV (C-C), 285.3 eV (C-O/C-N), 286.8 eV (C=O), and 287.8 eV (O-C=O), confirming the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups that enhance pseudocapacitive charge storage [41]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4c) reveals two characteristic peaks at 397.4 eV and 398.2 eV, corresponding to C-N and N-H bonding states, respectively [42]. These nitrogen species enhance charge transfer by modifying the electronic structure, increasing electron density near the Fermi level (as supported by DFT calculations). Combined with the quantitative analysis results from XPS, the samples NiCo-LDH, NiCo-LDH@CDs1.5, NiCo-LDH@CDs3.0, and NiCo-LDH@CDs4.5 contain N elements at concentrations of 4.57, 6.0, 6.92, and 5.56 %, respectively (Table S2). The increasing of N content in NiCo-LDH after compositing with TCDs is significant, corroborating the successful doping of N and the effective compositing of TCDs with NiCo-LDH. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4d) displays peaks at 530.2 eV (metal–oxygen bonds Ni-O and Co-O) [43], 531.2 eV (–OH), and 532.2 eV (adsorbed O2 molecules). The presence of –OH groups facilitates surface redox reactions, improving pseudocapacitive behaviour [44]. For Ni 2p high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 4e), in addition to the two satellite peaks at 878.5 eV and 860.6 eV, the two peaks at 872.4 eV and 854.8 eV correspond to Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2 spin–orbit peaks [45], with a spin–orbit peak separation of 17.6 eV, indicating that Ni exists as Ni(OH)2 in NiCo-LDH [46]. The high-resolution spectrum fitting results for Co 2p, as shown in Fig. 4f, feature a Co 2p1/2 spin–orbit peak at 796.3 eV that can be split into characteristic peaks attributed to Co3+ (795.4 eV) and Co2+ (796.6 eV) [47], and the spin–orbit peak at 780.9 eV can be split into those attributed to Co 2p3/2′s Co3+ (779.6 eV) and Co2+ (781.3 eV), indicating that Co exists in different valence states of Co(OH)2 in NiCo-LDH [48]. Further XPS analysis (Fig. 5 e-f) shows that the Ni 2p and Co 2p binding energies shift toward lower values, indicating a redistribution of charge density within the NiCo-LDH@TCDs structure. These electronic modifications corroborate the DFT-predicted charge carrier enhancement, demonstrating that TCDs alter the electronic structure to improve conductivity.

In summary, these physicochemical characterizations confirm that the NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite materials were successfully synthesized by incorporating tannin carbon dots through a hydrothermal process. Compared with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs), TCDs exhibit a higher degree of hydrophilicity and more chemically active sites, which significantly benefits their application in energy storage. The inclusion of tannin carbon dots not only altered the morphology and structural characteristics of the composites but also introduced a rich array of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. These modifications are expected to enhance the wettability and provide additional Faradaic reaction sites, potentially improving the electrochemical performance of the composites as supercapacitor electrode materials. To provide insights into the effects of TCDs on the structural attributes of NiCo-LDH, we conducted DFT calculations. Utilizing the relaxation-optimized crystal configurations (Fig. S3), we calculated the electronic density of states (DOS) of both NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites. Fig. 5a and b demonstrate that the DOS of both NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs traverse the Fermi level, suggesting their commendable electrical conductivity. Given the pivotal role of electron states proximal to the Fermi level in governing electron transfer processes, the DOS near this level has garnered considerable attention. Upon TCDs incorporation, the peak intensity of NiCo-LDH@TCDs near the Fermi level enhanced, thus increasing its conductivity. Furthermore, we undertook theoretical evaluations of the K-ion adsorption capabilities of both NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs. The results depicted in Fig. 5c and d highlight that NiCo-LDH@TCDs exhibits a stronger affinity (−2.15 eV) for K ions. Collectively, these results indicate that the introduction of TCDs not only improves the electronic structure of NiCo-LDH, but also enhances its affinity for K ions. Consequently, it is projected that electrodes constructed from NiCo-LDH@TCDs materials will manifest superior electrochemical performance, marked by elevated specific capacitance and reduced impedance.

To investigate the impact of varying TCD content on the electrochemical performance of the composites, we employed a 6 M KOH solution as the electrolyte in a standard three-electrode system. Fig. 6 a displays the CV curves of all electrode materials at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. Consistent with other reported LDH materials, all samples displayed a pair of distinct redox peaks, suggesting that the capacitive behavior is primarily driven by reversible redox reactions [49,50]. The GCD measurements at a current density of 1 A g−1 (Fig. 6b) revealed good symmetry without significant voltage drops, indicating strong reversibility of the redox reactions in the electrode materials [49]. Calculated specific capacitances for NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites with 1.5, 3.0, and 4.5 mg of TCDs at 1 A g−1 were 1204F g−1, 930.6F g−1, 1804.2F g−1, and 1324.4 F g−1, respectively. Remarkably, the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode achieved the highest specific capacitance (1804.2F g−1), significantly exceeding that of pure NiCo-LDH and surpassing most reported NiCo-LDH-based electrodes and other LDH composites [3,13,51]. The lower TCD loading (1.5 mg) led to limited functionalization and nanosheet agglomeration, while the higher TCD loading (4.5 mg) disrupted ion transport pathways, increasing charge transfer resistance. The 3.0 mg loading optimally enhanced ion diffusion and charge storage, yielding superior electrochemical performance. Fig. 6c and 6d present the CV and GCD results for the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode material at various scan rates (5–50 mV s−1) and current densities (1–10 A g−1). The CV curves retained their shape with increasing scan rates, and the GCD curves maintained symmetry even at higher current densities, indicating that the composite material exhibits excellent rate performance, underscoring its suitability as an electrode material [52]. The relationship between specific capacitance and current density calculated from the GCD curves at different current densities (Fig. 6e) demonstrates that, as the current density increased, the NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite material exhibited a capacitance retention rate far exceeding that of pure NiCo-LDH. The enhanced specific capacitance and rate performance of the NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite can be attributed to several synergistic effects introduced by the TCDs, especially for sample NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0. First, the high conductivity of carbon dots provides efficient electron transport pathways within the electrode, reducing internal resistance and facilitating rapid charge–discharge processes. This enhancement is crucial for maintaining high capacitance even at elevated current densities. This further support the DFT-predicted improvements in charge mobility. Second, the presence of carbon dots optimizes the pore structure of NiCo-LDH by increasing the surface area and creating a network of interconnected pores [11]. This structure provides greater electrochemical accessibility and facilitates efficient ion diffusion within the electrode. Moreover, the oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of TCDs increase the wettability of the composite, improving electrolyte penetration and ion transport. The nitrogen-doped sites also contribute additional pseudocapacitance through redox-active sites, further enhancing the specific capacitance [32,37,53]. Lastly, the carbon dots play a vital role in maintaining structural stability during cycling. By mitigating volume expansion and contraction in NiCo-LDH, they help preserve the composite’s integrity under high current densities, leading to improved cycling stability and rate capability.

The EIS spectra mainly consist of a semicircle in the high-frequency region representing charge transfer resistance (Rct) and a straight line in the low-frequency region representing diffusion resistance (Rw), with the intercept on the X-axis in the high-frequency region representing the internal resistance of the material and the electrolyte ion resistance (Rs) [54]. Fig. 6f displays the AC impedance test results of NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite electrode materials in the frequency range of 10 mHz-100 kHz, along with equivalent circuit fitting data (Table S3). All electrode materials exhibit low Rs values around 0.4 Ω, reflecting minimal series resistance, 0.36 Ω and 0.37 Ω for NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0, respectively. Notably, the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode displays the smallest Rct value (0.84 Ω V) and warburg impedance (Rw) (0.36 Ω) for NiCo-LDH@TCDs samples, and also lower than NiCo-LDH (1.13 Ω and 0.44 Ω, respetively), indicating that its unique regular needle-like spherical structure facilitates more efficient diffusion and transport of electrolyte ions, which contribute significantly to the electrode’s high specific capacitance. These result aligns with DFT calculations, which predict an increase in DOS near the Fermi level, facilitating more efficient electron transport. Additionally, the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) values for all samples are significantly lower than the pseudocapacitance (CPE) values, 0.0001F and 0.89F for NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0, reaffirming that the capacitive behavior of the materials is primarily pseudocapacitive, consistent with the CV and GCD results. Fig. 6h exhibit the capacitive contribution rates at of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 at the scanning rates of 20 mV s−1 (green area), where the capacitive and diffusion-controlled contributions are 30.89 % and 69.11 %, respectively, indicating that diffusion is the predominant mechanism in the charge storage process. The DFT results further support the experimental findings by providing insights into the electronic and structural enhancements imparted by TCDs. The increased DOS near the Fermi level for NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites suggests enhanced electronic conductivity, which facilitates rapid electron transfer and supports the high rate capability observed. The capacitive contributions at scan rates of 5, 10, 20, and 50 mV s−1 are 23 %, 24.01 %, 30.89 %, and 64.54 %, respectively (Fig. 6i). This trend confirms that the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 electrode is well-suited for high-rate Faradaic reactions, as it can efficiently conduct charge through both capacitive and diffusion-controlled processes, particularly at elevated scan rates. To evaluate the scalability of the synthesis method, a 5 × scale-up experiment was conducted for the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 sample. The electrochemical tests confirmed that the scaled-up material retained comparable electrochemical performance.
Electrochemical properties of ASC.

To evaluate the practical applicability of the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 composite electrode, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device was assembled using NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 as the positive electrode and tannin-based activated carbon (TAC600-4, see Fig. S4 for electrochemical results) as the negative electrode in a 6 M KOH electrolyte. Proper charge balancing between the positive and negative electrodes is crucial for maximizing energy density and preventing performance degradation. The mass ratio of the electrodes (m+/m−) was determined using the capacitance balance. To determine the maximum stable voltage window, CV and GCD tests were conducted across different operating voltages (0–1.0 V, 0–1.2 V, 0–1.4 V, and 0–1.6 V), as shown in Fig. 7 a-b. Even at 1.6 V, the CV curves showed no significant distortion, and GCD profiles maintained high symmetry, indicating efficient charge storage without severe polarization or side reactions [55]. However, operating beyond 1.6 V led to increased electrolyte degradation, reducing long-term stability. Fig. 7c displays the CV results of the ASC device at scan rates from 5-100 mV s−1; it is evident that the electrode’s CV curves maintained stable shapes even at high scan rates of up to 100 mV s−1, demonstrating the high rate performance of the electrode materials [2,56].

The GCD results of the ASC device at different current densities (Fig. 7d) show that the GCD curves maintained good symmetry, indicating good reversibility of the redox reactions occurring. The specific capacitances of the ASC device at current densities of 1 A g−1, 2 A g−1, 5 A g−1, and 10 A g−1 were calculated as 122.4F g−1, 109.5F g−1, 92.5F g−1, and 86.5F g−1, respectively. For comparison, replacing the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 composite with pure NiCo-LDH under identical conditions resulted in a significantly lower specific capacitance of 82F g−1 at 1 A g−1, highlighting the substantial performance enhancement achieved with the TCDs modification (Fig. S5). The energy and power densities of the ASC devices are displayed on a Ragone plot (Fig. 7e). The NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0//TAC600-4 device achieved an energy density of 87 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1, and notably, it maintained a high power density of 61.6 Wh kg−1 even at 8000 W kg−1, significantly outperforming the NiCo-LDH//TAC600-4 device (58 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1 and 41.9 Wh kg−1 at 8000 W kg−1). The Ragone plot (Fig. 7e) shows that the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0//TAC600-4 ASC outperforms most previously reported NiCo-LDH-based supercapacitors in terms of both energy and power density (Table 2 ). This superior performance can be attributed to the high electrical conductivity of NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 and the enhanced ion diffusion facilitated by the hierarchical porous structure. To assess long-term stability, the ASC device underwent a GCD cycle stability test at 10 A g−1 over 10,000 cycles, with results illustrated in Fig. 7f. The NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0//TAC600-4 device exhibited a capacitance retention of 78.6 % and coulombic efficiency of 100.9 % after 10,000 cycles, confirming its robustness and suitability for long-term supercapacitor applications. The DFT calculations further support these experimental findings, as the adsorption energy analysis highlights enhanced K-ion interaction at the active sites, effectively reducing ion aggregation and mitigating structural degradation during prolonged cycling. To further elucidate the degradation mechanisms and assess the structural integrity of the electrodes, post-cycling characterizations were conducted using Raman spectroscopy and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS). Post-cycling EIS analysis (Fig. S6) revealed a moderate increase in charge transfer resistance (Rct) and electrolyte diffusion resistance (Rs), accompanied by an increase in Warburg impedance (Rw). These increases indicate possible partial collapse of the porous structure or delamination between the TCDs and NiCo-LDH, which slightly hinders ion transport pathways and contributes to the observed performance degradation. Raman spectra (Fig. S7) further confirm structural degradation, which signifies the formation of additional defects and partial loss of graphitic order in the carbon network. This degradation is likely attributed to electrochemical instability, minor carbon framework breakdown, and deterioration of the NiCo-LDH structure during long-term cycling. Despite these structural changes, the NiCo-LDH@TCDs composite retained relatively high long-term stability, underscoring the critical role of TCDs in enhancing mechanical integrity, minimizing electrode deterioration, and prolonging the electrochemical lifespan of the device under repeated charge/discharge cycles. For a comprehensive comparison, Table 2 summarizes the energy density, power density, and cycle stability of the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0//TAC600-4 device relative to other reported ASC devices. It is evident that the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 composite offers competitive or superior performance across these key parameters of energy density, power density, and cycle stability, reinforcing its suitability for high-performance supercapacitors. To further demonstrate the practical applicability of the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0//TAC600-4 ASC device, a small-scale experiment was conducted to power a commercial LED light. As shown in the inset of Fig. 7f, the ASC device was successfully able to illuminate a blue LED for an extended period approximately 34 min. This demonstration highlights the potential of the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0//TAC600-4 ASC as a practical energy storage solution capable of powering low-power electronic devices.

The incorporation of nitrogen-doped tannin carbon dots (TCDs) into NiCo-LDH matrices has proven to be a highly effective strategy for enhancing both the structural stability and electrochemical performance of the composite electrodes. Both the experimental and theoretical results indicate that the addition of TCDs not only modifies the growth morphology of NiCo-LDH, leading to improved porosity and structural integrity, but also provides a greater density of redox-active sites, facilitating faster and more efficient charge transfer. These structural and compositional enhancements result in the NiCo-LDH@TCDs3.0 composite electrodes exhibiting a significantly higher specific capacitance and superior rate capability compared to pure NiCo-LDH. The asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) devices assembled using these composite electrodes achieved notably higher energy and power densities, highlighting the effectiveness of TCDs in boosting the performance of NiCo-LDH for practical applications. Additionally, the use of tannin-derived carbon dots (TCDs) from biomass waste enhances the eco-friendliness and commercial viability of this method. The presence of TCDs contributes to a marked improvement in cycling stability, allowing the device to retain substantial capacitance even under high-rate discharge conditions. Overall, this study presents a scalable and sustainable strategy for synthesizing NiCo-LDH@TCDs composites, demonstrating enhanced electrochemical performance through the synergistic effects of TCDs. The synthesis approach is cost-effective and industrially viable, making it suitable for large-scale production. Beyond asymmetric supercapacitors, the versatile electrochemical properties of NiCo-LDH@TCDs suggest potential applications in hybrid capacitors and battery systems, paving the way for next-generation energy storage technologies.
